slides.title: Hybrid Post-Quantum Password Authenticated Key Exchange
Silithium - A compact, efficient and non-separable hybrid signature
The Asynchronous Remote Key Generation (ARKG) algorithm
PQuAKE: Compact post-quantum mutual  authentication for key exchange

polls.text: Is the topic of Hybrid PQ/T Signatures something the CFRG should explore adopting?

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

Goodmorning ~vietnam~ CFRG

We really should meet in Vietnam some time.  Just for that.

chat.author: Kris Kwiatkowski
chat.text: :love_you_gesture:

chat.author: Pascal Schäfer
chat.text: Good morning

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: Good, hard rain yesterday in Detroit and dropped the temp and washed the smoke out...

chat.author: John Gray
chat.text: Robert:   Sorry about the smoke coming from my homeland...  Glad it is cleaned out for you now.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Is there a WG asking for FrodoKEM?

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: ChaCha20 and Poly1305 were not new, but at least the "glue" that stuck them together was sort-of novel. Doesn't mean it's a thing CFRG needs to do again.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

Is there a WG asking for FrodoKEM?

At least IPsecME. I think also TLS.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: There are un-adopted drafts in e.g. ipsecme referencing FrodoKEM

chat.author: Morgane Guerreau
chat.text: the FrodoKEM question was raised in ipsecme

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Maybe IETF should spin up a "crypto engineering" working group to write documents like that.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: IPsecME is definitely waiting for a not-behind-a-paywall specification.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: For which reason? Just to check a policy box or technical reasons?

chat.author: Pascal Schäfer
chat.text:
Yoav Nir said:

Maybe IETF should spin up a "crypto engineering" working group to write documents like that.

I think this idea came up already before some IETFs ago.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Because the IETF won't publish documents with a normative reference to a non-public document.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Pascal Schäfer said:

Yoav Nir said:

Maybe IETF should spin up a "crypto engineering" working group to write documents like that.

I think this idea came up already before some IETFs ago.

Review would be hard in such a working group. Who can check that the IETF "FrodoKEM" is the same as the ISO "FrodoKEM"?  Also strange that this WG would not have change control in any meaningful way.

chat.author: Dmitry Belyavskiy
chat.text: Shouldn't usability considerations and comparison be in PQUIP , not in CFRG? I totally agree we need such a document

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I don't think benchmarks are as useful because they're extremely platform-specific and tend to be a moving target. Though at high-level you can make some general observations

chat.author: Dmitry Belyavskiy
chat.text: Sure, no benchmarks

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: shameless plug: https://pqshield.github.io/nist-sigs-zoo/kems/

chat.author: Flo D
chat.text: +1 Scott

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: we can just use seeds for everything

chat.author: Tanja Lange
chat.text: Maybe it's easier with typing. I'm not suggesting to have a comparison document on security. I even said that I don't think that that is useful or possible. However, a CFRG document with comparison of the usability would be useful. I see @Thom Wiggers referencing the zoo. I'd also add https://bench.cr.yp.to/results-kem.html We have the numbers, but we're the (remaining) group for guidance on what crypto fits where.

chat.author: Flo D
chat.text: I agree that trying to iron out potential clashes between different WGs is one of the things PQUIP should have been doing.  It hasn't really worked out that way so far, but it doesn't mean it couldn't do that in the future.  It's also much less busy than CFRG.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I should add a link to EBACS, my benchmarks are much less comprehensive (though maybe slightly more accessible)

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: So since CFRG doesn't want to adopt draft-longa-cfrg-frodokem, maybe it's time to go to DISPATCH to see what the IETF can do with it.

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text: "Who can check that the IETF "FrodoKEM" is the same as the ISO "FrodoKEM"? " The individual draft authors are the ISO authors, I've asked them and they say it's the same modulo the ID has the 640 variants, and allows private key seed format (head-up LAMPS!).  I also have access to the ISO document and my team has confirmed that the ISO and ID are the same.

chat.author: Flo D
chat.text: Surely Dispatch will just send it back to CFRG?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: but if CFRG then refuses it at least we clarify that there is no forum / a lack of a forum?

chat.author: Flo D
chat.text: Fair point

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: CFRG has already refused. It's the same people in the IRTF and IETF

chat.author: Kris Kwiatkowski
chat.text: What's a point of standardizing FrodoKEM in IETF if it is already done in ISO?

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Daniel Van Geest said:

"Who can check that the IETF "FrodoKEM" is the same as the ISO "FrodoKEM"? " The individual draft authors are the ISO authors, I've asked them and they say it's the same modulo the ID has the 640 variants, and allows private key seed format (head-up LAMPS!).  I also have access to the ISO document and my team has confirmed that the ISO and ID are the same.

Good for them. But someone other than the authors needs to review.

chat.author: Pascal Schäfer
chat.text:
Kris Kwiatkowski said:

What's a point of standardizing FrodoKEM in IETF if it is already done in ISO?

I think IETF drafts cannot use those references if someone wants to use FrodoKEM.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Kris Kwiatkowski said:

What's a point of standardizing FrodoKEM in IETF if it is already done in ISO?

Because we can't use an ISO document as a normative reference.

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: Yes you can

chat.author: Frank Denis
chat.text: There’s also an implementation of Longfellow in zig

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: not that I'm recommending it

chat.author: Alexey Melnikov
chat.text: What Eliot said

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: OK, "can't" is too strong. But the IRSG and the community always push back strongly on normative references to documents behind paywalls.

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: But for independent submissions I would want to see SOME freely accessible specification.

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: The goal, after all, is widespread implementation

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text:
Kris Kwiatkowski said:

What's a point of standardizing FrodoKEM in IETF if it is already done in ISO?

so that it also exists as a standard by a well regarded standards organization?

chat.author: Kris Kwiatkowski
chat.text: so that's done

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: I think PQ ZKP is an important topic for the CFRG. I like the engineering that has been done for Long Fellow. I'm not sure it will be the ZKP scheme that is practical enough to deploy for many applications, but it's important to have something relatively simple worked out already just in case.

chat.author: Daniel Van Geest
chat.text:
Yoav Nir said:

But someone other than the authors needs to review.

Daniel Van Geest said:

I also have access to the ISO document and my team has confirmed that the ISO and ID are the same.

If I need to do this more formally I can.

chat.author: Rob Hunter
chat.text: For several years particular versions of  the Common Criteria standard has also been defined as an ISO standard. Mainly when a major number release was made. I wonder why there isn't an equivalent for FrodoKEM given the EU want adoption for its PQ migration strategy.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: The EU is not necessarily behind the push for FrodoKEM. That's just the French and Germans. Cryptography policy, in large part, is a member state issue.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: ML-KEM is also fine with all these countries even if they consider FrodoKEM to be more conservative/slightly preferred

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: I think IETF and IRTF should spend their time on solving international technical problems. If there is no technical need for another KEM, I think it's a waste of our time.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Identifying and removing blockers for PQC migration should be a priority. Technical issues are more fun to solve, policy issues are important too (but I don't think there are any policy issues truely blocking ML-KEM(-hybrids))

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: @Bas by "technical need" you mean diverse mathematical approach?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: (I don't think there's strong policy blockers anywhere at this moment--but don't want to close the door on them)

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Daniel Van Geest said:

I also have access to the ISO document and my team has confirmed that the ISO and ID are the same.
If I need to do this more formally I can.
That would be useful.  I might mention your name if/when I raise it on the Dispatch list.

chat.author: Alexey Melnikov
chat.text: meetecho: can you move camera to the speaker?

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
Thom Wiggers
chat.text: if someone does have that QC sitting next to them, please go to the mic

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

Eliot Lear said:

@Bas by "technical need" you mean diverse mathematical approach?

That could be an argument. I have not seen that one being made yet properly. So far it's quoting national regulatory bodies, which I do not think is a good argument for an international organization.

I'd go further. Having 5 options for something hinders adoption and interoperability. If I were a product manager, I'd adopt a wait-and-see approach until the market decides what is the algorithm to use.  NIST did us all a further by requiring that we implement one of their selections by a certain date.

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: @ThomWiggers:  Image of Schiller with that Sun machine sitting in his office @MIT running their Kerberos.

chat.author: Tanja Lange
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

Eliot Lear said:

@Bas by "technical need" you mean diverse mathematical approach?

That could be an argument. I have not seen that one being made yet properly. So far it's quoting national regulatory bodies, which I do not think is a good argument for an international organization.

NIST made it in going for a second KEM and the security considerations documents show different underlying security assumptions.
IMO, FrodoKEM is more different in security assumption from ML-KEM  than HQC (both have a quasi-cyclic structure). Clasisic McEliece is yet more different.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Sorry @Robert Moskowitz, I think I'm too young for that reference :sweat_smile: :baby:

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: Jeff often had fun stories around that system...

chat.author: Dan Harkins
chat.text: the comment was that since kemeleon already splits rho from the vector of coefficients then the BUA-sKEM is not necessary. Just do ML-KEM.KeyGen and then kemeleon.

chat.author: Dan Harkins
chat.text: in fact, since this draft explicitly splits off rho then it is not really compatible with kemeleon as currently written.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text:
Dan Harkins said:

the comment was that since kemeleon already splits rho from the vector of coefficients then the BUA-sKEM is not necessary. Just do ML-KEM.KeyGen and then kemeleon.

this feels like it should be discussed in papers? Or at least repeated on the mailing list

chat.author: Dan Harkins
chat.text: Yes, it should be discussed on the list.

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: "To be fair" comment -- wish more folks did that kind of thing.

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: Only a few bytes smaller.  Then won't help me.  Sigh.

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text:
The IPSECME FrodoKEM draft-ietf-ipsecme-hybrid-kem-ikev2-frodo is adopted and WGLC was discussed. 
While nothing forbids the IETF to normatively reference paywalled cryptography, we really should not.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I think some SUF-CMA scheme would be good to have in the toolbox for places where signatures are used more arbitarily, e.g. the ways that SSH keys are (ab)used right now...

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: But it should probably be done quickly if it's worth doing, being available too late will make the point moot

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text:
I think an important property missing is the number of hash algorithm primitives in the composite.
Also I think they are all composites. That term should not be reserved for the 18 LAMPS algorithms

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: we should align with the PQUIP terminology draft

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: s/draft/rfc/ now :)

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: please eat microphone

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: (good enough now)

chat.author: Britta Hale
chat.text: There are multiple security nuances and hidden security failure modes for hybrid signatures that are not issues for hybrid KEMs. The presentation touched on  two of those, but serious discussion on all of them should occur before moving out on choice or even a decision to pursue such a draft at all.

chat.author: Morgane Guerreau
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

I think some SUF-CMA scheme would be good to have in the toolbox for places where signatures are used more arbitarily, e.g. the ways that SSH keys are (ab)used right now...

would it make sense to have one of those SUF-CMA schemes standardized directly into the SSH WG (or any WG where SUF-CMA has a strong case) if CFRG is not interested by the topic?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: maybe? That's up to sshm (and there may be charter issues because the outside-of-ssh uses of keys are technically out of scope of that working group...)

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Wait, why does SSH need SUF-CMA?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: SSH itself doesn't, but SSH public keys are used for e.g. code signing and in other places

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: Some of the SSH security proofs require SUF-CMA.

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: (or assume)

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: probably mostly a proof artifact

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: Probably.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/684 is EUF-CMA in any case

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: But I can get the sentiment of "we're sending these keys into the world, better make them as robust as possible."  (but also preferably sooner rather than later)

chat.author: Kris Kwiatkowski
chat.text:
Britta Hale said:

There are multiple security nuances and hidden security failure modes for hybrid signatures that are not issues for hybrid KEMs. The presentation touched on  two of those, but serious discussion on all of them should occur before moving out on choice or even a decision to pursue such a draft at all.

+1. On top of the security nuances, we already have a bloat of composite constructions (18 in LAMPS alone, plus other proposals). Before adding yet another, use cases and required properties should be stated on the list, so we can check whether existing schemes cover them.

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: @Thom:  Hmm.  That's unclear.  2025/684 is based on 2013/813 which does technically assume SUF-CMA.

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: The ACM CCS 2014 version used the term EUF-CMA, but the definition was actually SUF-CMA.

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: It was corrected in the ePrint version.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: The game 4 part in the proof of Lemma 4.1 is EUF-CMA—not SUF-CMA

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: But the signatures used with ssh today aren't suf afaik

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: In particular ECDSA allows you to swap the sign

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Bas Westerbaan actually I think they may also have a wrong definition

chat.author: Peter Campbell
chat.text: This is a restatement of the Game 3 in the proof of Lemma 1 from 2013/813, which does assume SUF-CMA.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Game 4 aborts if it receives a signature which was never output of a session with a matching session identifier. If signatures are malleable (e.g. append a byte), this is trivial to trigger.

chat.author: John Gray
chat.text: In regards to the bloat of composite signatures...  that was an artifact of so many voices in LAMPS asking for specific combinations.  Maybe we should have been more forceful in saying No.  Sorry...   The construction in LAMPS composite signatures is essentially fixed, the algorithms themselves are like parameters fixed by the OID.  We did try numerous times to prune the list, so on a positive note, it is smaller than it could have been.  :)    I think at one point their had been 32 of them!

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Now, I think this may simply be phrased incorrectly. SUF-CMA is typically necessary due to some partnering definition shenanigans but seems to not be the case here. So I think that the game can be rephrased that Game 4 aborts if it accepts without a partner. this is what the second paragraph in Game 4 describes anyway.

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text:
Sophie Schmieg wrote:

But the signatures used with ssh today aren't suf afaik
In particular ECDSA allows you to swap the sign

I think standardizing ECDSA with a trivial malleability vulnerability was a mistake.
SSH widely use EdDSA. The standardized versions of EdDSA are not malleable.

chat.author: Kris Kwiatkowski
chat.text:
John Gray said:

In regards to the bloat of composite signatures...  that was an artifact of so many voices in LAMPS asking for specific combinations.  Maybe we should have been more forceful in saying No.  Sorry...   The construction in LAMPS composite signatures is essentially fixed, the algorithms themselves are like parameters fixed by the OID.  We did try numerous times to prune the list, so on a positive note, it is smaller than it could have been.  :)    I think at one point their had been 32 of them!

I think we must begin with the real use cases that genuinely require hybrid signatures. Aiming to satisfy all conceivable ones will inevitably lead to an explosion in the number of algorithms.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Not sure how much it's used in practice, but the PGP signature variants of SSH seem malleable because of the unhashed signature subpackets

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: More practically, I'd rather see the paper revisited on whether SUF is actually required, before open the can of worms that's SUF hybrid signatures

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: for SSH itself I strongly doubt it cares about malleability

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: (of signatures, i.e., EUF-CMA is enough)

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: +1, I would be very surprised if SUF was needed

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I sent a message to Benjamin Dowling (who I think is also around in Vienna)

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: thank you!

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: Nice work from the chairs for clarifying and steering discussion
